query: shallow copy vs deep copy javascript interview question

competitors.label: MDN Shallow copy
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for shallow-copy semantics and points directly at the nested-reference behavior this prompt is about.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on what a shallow copy is and why nested references remain shared.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on state-management bugs, JSON-clone pitfalls, and when structuredClone is the safer production choice.

realWorldUseCases: Most bugs around state mutation come from thinking you made a deep copy but actually making a shallow one.
actionableExamples: Common ways to make shallow copies:
followUpConfusion: It silently breaks or loses: - undefined, Symbol, functions - Date, Map, Set, RegExp - Circular references (throws)

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines the concept correctly, but our page is more explicit about the real bug pattern developers hit when nested state is still shared after a copy.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still provide strong reference examples, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page goes further on the follow-ups interviewers usually ask next: JSON stringify clone limits, structuredClone, and why a shallow copy is often mistaken for full immutability.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny nested React or Angular state example later if we need more example separation over the docs.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical definition source, but our page is stronger for interview use because it turns copy semantics into concrete mutation and cloning failures.
